original_hypothesis.title: Protocol-wide denial of service when variablePoolBorrowRate becomes stale and offers use non-zero marketRateMultipliers
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: YieldCurveLibrary.getAdjustedAPR() reverts with STALE_RATE if marketRateMultiplier != 0 and either stale interval is 0 or the last update is older than the interval. This can halt market-order matching and APR querying for offers that depend on the variable rate. An attacker cannot force staleness directly, but can amplify impact by creating/encouraging offer book reliance on non-zero multipliers; once BORROW_RATE_UPDATER_ROLE misses updates (ops outage), large segments of the market become non-functional.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a practical operations risk: a single trusted updater failure can brick critical user flows that read APR in validation. Needs validation of how often multipliers are used and whether stale interval is non-zero in production.
deployment_assumptions: variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval is set to a finite value or left at 0
BORROW_RATE_UPDATER_ROLE updates can lapse due to operational outage
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Deploy.sol sets variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval to 0 in both setupLocal and setupProduction, implying default deployments disable the variable-rate feature unless updated later (script/Deploy.sol: setupProduction assigns o.variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval = 0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: getAdjustedAPR(): if marketRateMultiplier != 0 and (staleInterval==0 OR now-updatedAt>staleInterval) revert Errors.STALE_RATE

preconditions: At least one offer curve has marketRateMultipliers[i] != 0
A taker attempts buyCreditMarket/sellCreditMarket against such an offer (or tries to read APR via view functions used by integrators)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Offer validation calls APR computation which can revert due to staleness, preventing matching.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires BORROW_RATE_UPDATER_ROLE uptime and appropriate stale interval configuration.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During updater outages or when interval remains 0 post-deploy.

operational_questions: Is the stale interval intended to remain 0 in production, and are non-zero multipliers expected to be used?
What proportion of the order book would become unmatchable if stale updates occur?
Are there monitoring/alerting and fallback procedures for stale-rate events?
validation_requirements: Check production config for variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval and last updatedAt
Inspect typical offer curves for non-zero multipliers
Chaos test: simulate updater outage and quantify failed matches

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Seed the market with attractive offers that include non-zero multipliers (if participants rely on them).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for rate updates to become stale (or remain disabled with interval==0).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Trigger/observe widespread reverts on market-order validation, causing DoS and potentially forcing users into less favorable paths.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: No on-chain coordination needed, but depends on operational staleness occurrence.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High: matching and APR discovery can halt for offers using multipliers; integrators may treat reverts as protocol downtime.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low-to-medium: restore updater + adjust stale interval; but user experience and liquidity can be damaged.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Market-order matching and APR queries for variable-rate-dependent offers.

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Low to exploit effects; higher to strategically shape offer book reliance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverts with Errors.STALE_RATE in tx traces; no recent VariablePoolBorrowRateUpdated events.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden drop in matched volume; APR view calls reverting for common tenors.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Order book shifts to curves with marketRateMultiplier==0 during incidents.

validation_result.impact_summary: When variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval is 0 (the default), any offer with non-zero marketRateMultipliers will always revert on matching. If interval is non-zero, updater outages cause the same DoS.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The code defect exists at the intersection of three facts: (1) YieldCurveLibrary.getAdjustedAPR() reverts with STALE_RATE when marketRateMultiplier != 0 and staleRateInterval == 0; (2) the default deployment sets variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval = 0; (3) the protocol allows users to create offers with non-zero marketRateMultipliers without any validation against the stale rate interval configuration. This means offers with non-zero multipliers are permanently broken in the default deployment. Even if the admin later sets a non-zero interval, any lapse in BORROW_RATE_UPDATER_ROLE updates causes the same revert for all such offers. The BuyCreditLimit.s.sol deployment script itself uses marketRateMultipliers of 1e18, demonstrating the expectation that this feature should work.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence confirms the defect. YieldCurveLibrary.sol lines 65-73 show the revert path when staleRateInterval==0 and marketRateMultiplier!=0. Deploy.sol setupProduction sets variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval=0. validateYieldCurve has 'validate marketRateMultipliers // N/A' confirming no validation. The BuyCreditLimit.s.sol script uses non-zero multipliers, proving intended usage.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval == 0 (the default), the first branch of the else-if evaluates to true for ANY non-zero marketRateMultiplier, causing an unconditional revert. This makes all offers using market-rate-linked pricing permanently broken in default configuration.
Default production deployment sets staleRateInterval to 0, which combined with getAdjustedAPR logic means any offer with non-zero marketRateMultipliers will always revert.

No validation is performed on marketRateMultipliers during offer creation. Users can freely set non-zero values that will cause permanent reverts when matched, with no warning or guard against the stale-rate configuration.

calling_sequence: getAPR calls getAdjustedAPR(apr, marketRateMultiplier, params) for interpolation points
getAdjustedAPR: marketRateMultiplier != 0 -> checks staleRateInterval == 0 -> reverts STALE_RATE
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When a lender calls buyCreditMarket against a borrower whose borrowOffer has non-zero marketRateMultipliers, the validation step calls getAPRByTenor -> getAPR -> getAdjustedAPR. In getAdjustedAPR (YieldCurveLibrary.sol:65-73), since marketRateMultiplier != 0 and params.variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval == 0 (default), the condition on line 68 evaluates to true and reverts with STALE_RATE. The same path applies to sellCreditMarket, liquidateWithReplacement, getBorrowOfferAPR, and getLoanOfferAPR.

deployment_assumptions_checked: BORROW_RATE_UPDATER_ROLE updates can lapse due to operational outage
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.sol setupProduction (line ~90-91) explicitly sets variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval = 0 for both setupLocal and setupProduction. BuyCreditLimit.s.sol script uses marketRateMultipliers of 1e18, confirming expected usage of non-zero multipliers. No deployment script calls setVariablePoolBorrowRate or sets a non-zero stale interval post-deployment.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The defect does not require an active attacker. In the default deployment (staleRateInterval=0), any user who creates an offer with non-zero marketRateMultipliers will find their offer permanently unmatchable. Other users attempting to match against it will have their transactions revert. If the admin later sets a non-zero stale interval and the BORROW_RATE_UPDATER_ROLE stops updating, the same DoS occurs for all offers with non-zero multipliers. An attacker could amplify the impact by encouraging market participants to use non-zero multipliers (which is the expected use case per deployment scripts), then waiting for an updater outage.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH - In default configuration, the market-rate-linked pricing feature is completely non-functional. Any offer using non-zero marketRateMultipliers will cause reverts on buyCreditMarket, sellCreditMarket, liquidateWithReplacement, and view functions (getBorrowOfferAPR, getLoanOfferAPR). This prevents matching of a potentially large segment of the order book. Even with correct configuration (non-zero interval), any updater downtime causes the same DoS. The BuyCreditLimit.s.sol script demonstrates that non-zero multipliers ARE expected to be used.
recommended_mitigations: Add validation in validateYieldCurve to reject non-zero marketRateMultipliers when variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval == 0
Require variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval > 0 during initialization if the protocol intends to support market-rate-linked offers
Add a validation check during offer creation (buyCreditLimit/sellCreditLimit) that verifies the variable pool borrow rate is not stale for offers with non-zero marketRateMultipliers
Consider treating staleRateInterval == 0 as 'no staleness check' rather than 'always stale' to avoid the permanent DoS in default configuration
Implement monitoring and alerting for stale variable pool borrow rates and automate updater failover
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Size with default config (staleRateInterval=0). 2. Create a borrow offer with non-zero marketRateMultipliers via sellCreditLimit. 3. Deposit borrow tokens as a lender. 4. Attempt buyCreditMarket against the borrower's offer. 5. Observe STALE_RATE revert. 6. Verify getBorrowOfferAPR also reverts for the same offer.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol team intends to always update variablePoolBorrowRateStaleRateInterval post-deployment before any market activity (not evidenced in scripts)
Whether non-zero marketRateMultipliers are expected to be used in production or only in testing
additional_testing_needed: End-to-end test with default config showing offer creation with non-zero multipliers followed by failed matching
Test with configured stale interval showing normal operation followed by updater outage causing the same DoS
Measure what percentage of realistic order book configurations would use non-zero marketRateMultipliers
